first_paragraph: **April Janet Pearson**[[1]](#cite_note-1) (born 23 January 1989) is an English actress. Born and brought up in [Bristol](/wiki/Bristol "Bristol"), Pearson was drawn to acting from a young age and appeared in local theatre productions as a child. She made her acting debut in 1998 at the age of nine on the British [medical drama](/wiki/Medical_drama "Medical drama") series *[Casualty](/wiki/Casualty_(TV_series) "Casualty (TV series)")*, and earned wider recognition in her breakthrough role as teenager [Michelle Richardson](/wiki/Michelle_Richardson "Michelle Richardson") in the [E4](/wiki/E4_(TV_channel) "E4 (TV channel)") teen drama series *[Skins](/wiki/Skins_(British_TV_series) "Skins (British TV series)")* (2007–2008),[[2]](#cite_note-2) for which she was nominated for the [Golden Nymph Award](/wiki/Golden_Nymph_Award "Golden Nymph Award") for an Outstanding Actress in a Drama Series at the [Monte-Carlo Television Festival](/wiki/Monte-Carlo_Television_Festival "Monte-Carlo Television Festival").[[3]](#cite_note-Nymph-3)

**April Janet Pearson**[[1]](#cite_note-1) (born 23 January 1989) is an English actress. Born and brought up in [Bristol](/wiki/Bristol "Bristol"), Pearson was drawn to acting from a young age and appeared in local theatre productions as a child. She made her acting debut in 1998 at the age of nine on the British [medical drama](/wiki/Medical_drama "Medical drama") series *[Casualty](/wiki/Casualty_(TV_series) "Casualty (TV series)")*, and earned wider recognition in her breakthrough role as teenager [Michelle Richardson](/wiki/Michelle_Richardson "Michelle Richardson") in the [E4](/wiki/E4_(TV_channel) "E4 (TV channel)") teen drama series *[Skins](/wiki/Skins_(British_TV_series) "Skins (British TV series)")* (2007–2008),[[2]](#cite_note-2) for which she was nominated for the [Golden Nymph Award](/wiki/Golden_Nymph_Award "Golden Nymph Award") for an Outstanding Actress in a Drama Series at the [Monte-Carlo Television Festival](/wiki/Monte-Carlo_Television_Festival "Monte-Carlo Television Festival").[[3]](#cite_note-Nymph-3)

She later made her film debut when cast in [Jon Wright](/wiki/Jon_Wright "Jon Wright")'s [comedy horror](/wiki/Comedy_horror "Comedy horror") [slasher](/wiki/Slasher_film "Slasher film") film *[Tormented](/wiki/Tormented_(2009_British_film) "Tormented (2009 British film)")* (2009). Pearson followed this with her [Bristol Old Vic](/wiki/Bristol_Old_Vic "Bristol Old Vic") debut as Jemma in [Catherine Johnson](/wiki/Catherine_Johnson_(playwright) "Catherine Johnson (playwright)")'s *Suspension* (2009); the production centering around the [Clifton Suspension Bridge](/wiki/Clifton_Suspension_Bridge "Clifton Suspension Bridge") and the nearby [Avon Gorge Hotel](/wiki/Clifton_Rocks_Railway "Clifton Rocks Railway"),[[4]](#cite_note-4) and as Callie in Rachel Sternberg and Jemma Wayne's Negative Space (2009), in which she received praise for both performances.

Her transition to horror and independent films began with the 2016 horror film *Fractured*, then followed by *Caught* (2017), for which she garnered critical acclaim for playing Mrs. Blair, one half of the antagonistic couple.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Her role of Amy in *Dark Beacon* (2017) earned her two Best Actress awards at American Horror and Upstate NY Horror [film festivals](/wiki/Film_festivals "Film festivals").[[6]](#cite_note-6) She followed this by her portrayal of Isla Crane in [Edgar Wallace](/wiki/Edgar_Wallace "Edgar Wallace")'s *The Case of the Frightened Lady* at the [Theatre Royal, Windsor](/wiki/Theatre_Royal,_Windsor "Theatre Royal, Windsor"), in which she received praise for her performance.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=April_Pearson&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Pearson was born and raised in [Bristol](/wiki/Bristol "Bristol").[[8]](#cite_note-8) She attended [Colston's Girls' School](/wiki/Colston%27s_Girls%27_School "Colston's Girls' School") in the [Montpelier](/wiki/Montpelier,_Bristol "Montpelier, Bristol") area of Bristol, where in 2007 she was appointed [head girl](/wiki/Head_girl_and_head_boy "Head girl and head boy"),[[9]](#cite_note-Montpelier-9) and was a member of the [Bristol Old Vic Young Company](/wiki/Bristol_Old_Vic#Engagement_and_Young_Company "Bristol Old Vic").[[10]](#cite_note-10) Both of her parents had previously worked in the [television industry](/wiki/Television_industry "Television industry"), with her father still being involved in the British medical drama series *[Casualty](/wiki/Casualty_(TV_series) "Casualty (TV series)")*.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-Theatre-12)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=April_Pearson&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

### Early career and *Skins* (1998–2008)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=April_Pearson&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early career and Skins (1998–2008)")]

Pearson joined a theatre group, located in Bristol, becoming one of its founding members at three years of age.[[12]](#cite_note-Theatre-12) Throughout her youth, she starred in various plays with both her drama group and whilst at school.[[12]](#cite_note-Theatre-12) At the age of 9, Pearson made her television debut with a minor role in the British [medical drama](/wiki/Medical_drama "Medical drama") series *[Casualty](/wiki/Casualty_(TV_series) "Casualty (TV series)")* in 1998.[[13]](#cite_note-Theatre_Royal_Editors-13) Pearson was a student at [Colston's Girls' School](/wiki/Colston%27s_Girls%27_School "Colston's Girls' School") in 2006 when she was cast in the television teenage-drama *[Skins](/wiki/Skins_(British_TV_series) "Skins (British TV series)")*.[[12]](#cite_note-Theatre-12) [Casting director](/wiki/Casting_director "Casting director") Jane Ripley and [Adam Smith](/wiki/Adam_Smith_(director) "Adam Smith (director)") travelled to Colston's Girls' School to hold auditions, wherein Ripley asked Pearson whether she wished to audition after watching her drama class.[[9]](#cite_note-Montpelier-9)[[14]](#cite_note-14) The programme was a success and ran for seven series, only two of which Pearson appeared in.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Her performance was well received; the character was popular, and Pearson garnered widespread attention.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) She stated that the amusing nature of simply playing someone who's very different from her in person was what drew her to playing Richardson in *Skins*.[[18]](#cite_note-18) After the release of the first season, Pearson stated that being recognised was rather strange, though remarked that, "I try to be nice to everyone and hope they keep watching the show."[[19]](#cite_note-19) *Skins* won the [British Academy of Film and Television Arts](/wiki/British_Academy_of_Film_and_Television_Arts "British Academy of Film and Television Arts") (BAFTA) Philip Audience Award, and Pearson was nominated for the [Golden Nymph Award](/wiki/Golden_Nymph_Award "Golden Nymph Award") for Best Actress in a Drama Series.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[3]](#cite_note-Nymph-3) Of her time on *Skins*, Pearson spoke of how she learnt to cope on a set and how to act in the working world, to which she added: "I owe the position I am in now to *Skins* and I'm so happy and proud that it has done so well."[[22]](#cite_note-22)

### *Tormented* and Bristol Old Vic debut (2009–2012)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=April_Pearson&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Tormented and Bristol Old Vic debut (2009–2012)")]

Pearson briefly appeared as Karen Shevlin an episode of the British [medical drama](/wiki/Medical_drama "Medical drama") series *Casualty*.[[23]](#cite_note-23) She later made her feature film debut as sadistic schoolgirl Natasha Cummings, in [Jon Wright](/wiki/Jon_Wright "Jon Wright")'s [comedy horror](/wiki/Comedy_horror "Comedy horror") [slasher](/wiki/Slasher_film "Slasher film") film *[Tormented](/wiki/Tormented_(2009_British_film) "Tormented (2009 British film)")* (2009).[[24]](#cite_note-24) She reunited with her *Skins* co-star [Larissa Wilson](/wiki/Larissa_Wilson "Larissa Wilson"),[[25]](#cite_note-25) and starred opposite [Alex Pettyfer](/wiki/Alex_Pettyfer "Alex Pettyfer"), [Tuppence Middleton](/wiki/Tuppence_Middleton "Tuppence Middleton") and [Georgia King](/wiki/Georgia_King "Georgia King").[[26]](#cite_note-26)
In an interview with *[The Evening Standard](/wiki/The_Evening_Standard "The Evening Standard")*'s Andy Barker, she stated, "I don't want to pigeon-hole myself this early on. I enjoy every genre."[[27]](#cite_note-Tormented-27)

Later in 2009, Pearson returned to theatre starring in [Catherine Johnson](/wiki/Catherine_Johnson_(playwright) "Catherine Johnson (playwright)")'s *Suspension* (2009) at the [Bristol Old Vic](/wiki/Bristol_Old_Vic "Bristol Old Vic").[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29) [Michael Billington](/wiki/Michael_Billington_(critic) "Michael Billington (critic)") wrote, in his review for *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")*, "You sense a bond of buried affection between James Lailey's guilt-ridden Gerry and [Pearson] as the bridling bride-to-be. In fact, all the performances from director Heather Williams's largely Bristol-based cast are good."[[30]](#cite_note-30) *[Metro](/wiki/Metro_(British_newspaper) "Metro (British newspaper)")* described it as "a fantastic success – for Johnson, the cast and crew, for Bristol Old Vic and for Bristol, the star of the show."[[31]](#cite_note-31) [Paul Callan](/wiki/Paul_Callan "Paul Callan") of *[The Daily Express](/wiki/The_Daily_Express "The Daily Express")* wrote: "Down in the hotel Jemma panics with her pre-wedding nerves and the (very pretty) [Pearson] gives us all too recognisable Big Day nerves. She has a fine sense of comic timing and is a perfect foil for the overpowering Anita, her Sherman Tank of a mother."[[32]](#cite_note-32) Additionally, in the autumn, she portrayed kidnapped teenager called Callie in Rachel Sternberg and Jemma Wayne's *Negative Space* (2009).[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35) *[The Evening Standard](/wiki/The_Evening_Standard "The Evening Standard")* spoke highly of Pearson's performance, in which they wrote: "The star, emphatically, is [Pearson], gamine and effulgent as Callie. Pearson is 20, but proves unsettlingly convincing as a girl not much more than half her age."[[36]](#cite_note-36) In 2011, Pearson returned to *Casualty* to star as Grace Fitch,[[37]](#cite_note-37) whilst starring in David Allain's [short film](/wiki/Short_film "Short film") *Will You Marry Me?* (2011).[[38]](#cite_note-38) In 2012, Pearson co-starred in Jen Moss' [dark](/wiki/Black_comedy "Black comedy") [comedy-drama](/wiki/Comedy-drama "Comedy-drama") short film *My Brother's Keeper (Or How Not To Survive The Apocalypse)*, playing the role of Jess, who is looking after her well-meaning but dim-witted brother during a [zombie apocalypse](/wiki/Zombie_apocalypse "Zombie apocalypse").[[39]](#cite_note-39) Moss would later be awarded the Best Writer and Director Awards at the [Viscera Film Festival](/wiki/Viscera_Film_Festival "Viscera Film Festival").[[40]](#cite_note-Keeper-40) Of Pearson's performance as Jess, *[Bloody Disgusting](/wiki/Bloody_Disgusting "Bloody Disgusting")* remarked that the dynamic between Alex Esmail and Pearson is both "touching and funny".[[40]](#cite_note-Keeper-40)

### Theatre return and collaboration with Jamie Patterson (2013–2015)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=April_Pearson&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Theatre return and collaboration with Jamie Patterson (2013–2015)")]

She later reunited with Skins co-creator [Bryan Elsley](/wiki/Bryan_Elsley "Bryan Elsley") in 2013, in which she briefly appeared as Liz in *[Dates](/wiki/Dates_(TV_series) "Dates (TV series)")*.[[41]](#cite_note-41) She also appeared in television programmes *Casualty*, and *[Comedy Feeds](/wiki/Comedy_Feeds "Comedy Feeds")*, before appearing in a minor role in the [Giles Foster](/wiki/Giles_Foster "Giles Foster")'s television film *[Unknown Heart](/w/index.php?title=Unknown_Heart&action=edit&redlink=1 "Unknown Heart (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/De_c%C5%93ur_inconnu "fr:De cœur inconnu")]* (2014).[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43) Pearson also appeared as Gen in *Circle of Truth*, a short film shot by David Allain which explored some of the challenges that people with [multiple sclerosis](/wiki/Multiple_sclerosis "Multiple sclerosis") face when deciding whether or not to disclose their diagnosis to their colleagues.[[44]](#cite_note-44) The film's inception came about when *Shift.ms*, a charity and social network for people with Multiple Sclerosis, contacted producer Jess Gormley.[[45]](#cite_note-Sclerosis-45) Alongside Pearson, *Circle of Truth* featured [Ashley Thomas](/wiki/Bashy "Bashy"), [Nathan McMullen](/wiki/Nathan_McMullen "Nathan McMullen"), [Lauren Socha](/wiki/Lauren_Socha "Lauren Socha"), [Simon Day](/wiki/Simon_Day "Simon Day"), [Justin Edwards](/wiki/Justin_Edwards_(actor) "Justin Edwards (actor)"), and [Nicholas Burns](/wiki/Nicholas_Burns_(actor) "Nicholas Burns (actor)").[[45]](#cite_note-Sclerosis-45) In April 2014, Pearson co-starred opposite [Ben Peel](/wiki/Ben_Peel "Ben Peel") in Greg Zinger's [romantic comedy](/wiki/Romantic_comedy "Romantic comedy") short film *The Engagement* (2014), which focused on the difficulty of making marital commitment.[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47) In her second feature-film, Pearson portrayed Beth Prince, a young woman in search of her happy ever after, in Jamie Patterson's romantic comedy *Home for Christmas* (2014).[[48]](#cite_note-48) The film was based on [Cally Taylor](/wiki/Cally_Taylor "Cally Taylor")'s novel of the same name, and co-starred [Lucy Griffiths](/wiki/Lucy_Griffiths_(actress,_born_1986) "Lucy Griffiths (actress, born 1986)"), [Karl Davies](/wiki/Karl_Davies "Karl Davies"), [Derren Nesbitt](/wiki/Derren_Nesbitt "Derren Nesbitt"), and [Shirley Jaffe](/wiki/Shirley_Jaffe_(actress) "Shirley Jaffe (actress)").[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50) Later that year, Pearson reunited with David Allain to reprise her role of Gen in short film *Working It Out*, in which her character interviews other people with multiple sclerosis about their experiences of employment.[[51]](#cite_note-Gen-51) The soundtrack was provided by [The Maccabees](/wiki/The_Maccabees_(band) "The Maccabees (band)"), and Pearson co-stars alongside Gabriel Bisset-Smith and [Preeya Kalidas](/wiki/Preeya_Kalidas "Preeya Kalidas").[[51]](#cite_note-Gen-51)

In January 2015, she starred in the music video for [The Wombats](/wiki/The_Wombats "The Wombats")' single, "[Greek Tragedy](/wiki/Greek_Tragedy_(song) "Greek Tragedy (song)")", in which Pearson plays an obsessed Wombats fan.[[52]](#cite_note-52) In the first half of 2015, Pearson played Lexi in Neil Jones's critically panned [action film](/wiki/Action_film "Action film") *[Age of Kill](/wiki/Age_of_Kill "Age of Kill")* (2015).[[53]](#cite_note-53) In the same year, Pearson starred in Nick Gillespie's [psychological horror film](/wiki/Psychological_horror "Psychological horror") *[Tank 432](/wiki/Tank_432 "Tank 432")* (2015).[[54]](#cite_note-54) Although the film was critically panned, praise was given to its cast.[[55]](#cite_note-55) Emilie Black of *Cinema Crazed* praised Pearson's Annabella, whilst writing "it was nice to see a female character not in need of saving, who is just one of the guys, in a film involving a military-style team and tough guys."[[56]](#cite_note-56) Frank Scheck of *[The Hollywood Reporter](/wiki/The_Hollywood_Reporter "The Hollywood Reporter")* acknowledged that "Several talented performers are wasted in the morass."[[57]](#cite_note-57) Ben Robins of *[The National Student](/wiki/The_National_Student "The National Student")* wrote: "Even the cast, a who’s who of largely forgotten-about (but still entirely reliable) British talent, from [Rupert Evans](/wiki/Rupert_Evans "Rupert Evans") to [Michael Smiley](/wiki/Michael_Smiley "Michael Smiley") and Skins’ [Pearson], seemed like a stab in the right direction."[[58]](#cite_note-58) Chuck Wilson wrote, in his review for *[The Village Voice](/wiki/The_Village_Voice "The Village Voice")*, "Trapping his cast in a tight space is daring, but Gillespie hasn't given his six good actors, among them Evans and [Gordon Kennedy](/wiki/Gordon_Kennedy_(actor) "Gordon Kennedy (actor)"), much to say beyond shouted variations on the word “fuck.”"[[59]](#cite_note-59) In 2015, Pearson returned to theatre to perform on stage in *Threesome* at the [Brighton Fringe](/wiki/Brighton_Fringe "Brighton Fringe"), and then the [Edinburgh Festival Fringe](/wiki/Edinburgh_Festival_Fringe "Edinburgh Festival Fringe").[[60]](#cite_note-60) The production received favourable reviews, with particularly strong praise for Pearson and her co-stars;[[61]](#cite_note-61)[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63) "Pearson, meanwhile, carries the part of the confident, daring and over-sexed third party to the limits – but successfully so. The dialogue is fast and furious, and with pacy interactions, the three actors together create a thoroughly watchable story".[[64]](#cite_note-64)

### Transition to horror and independent films (2016–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=April_Pearson&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Transition to horror and independent films (2016–present)")]

In 2016, Pearson starred in *Fractured*, about a couple whose peaceful getaway weekend goes awry, where she reunited with *Home for Christmas*'s [Karl Davies](/wiki/Karl_Davies "Karl Davies") and director Jamie Patterson.[[65]](#cite_note-65)[[66]](#cite_note-66) The cast of *Fractured* also included [Louisa Lytton](/wiki/Louisa_Lytton "Louisa Lytton"), [Jordan Metcalfe](/wiki/Jordan_Metcalfe "Jordan Metcalfe") and [Calvin Dean](/wiki/Calvin_Dean "Calvin Dean").[[67]](#cite_note-67) British horror genre [website](/wiki/Website "Website") *Love Horror* wrote: "Pearson and Davies are well suited to their characters, convincingly performing the loving couple that are being pursued."[[68]](#cite_note-68) For her work in *Fractured*, Pearson received the Best Actress Award from the [British Horror Film Festival](/wiki/British_Horror_Film_Festival "British Horror Film Festival").[[69]](#cite_note-bhff2016-69) She also starred as Gemma in Mdhamiri Á Nkemi's short film *Cuttings* (2016), in which her character returns to her family home after a recent death to discover buried family truths in an uncovered archive of home video.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

Pearson reunited with Patterson once again for *Caught* (2018), in which she plays Mrs. Blair, alongside Cian Barry's Mr. Blair, respectively.[[71]](#cite_note-71) The story follows a journalist couple who invite a man and woman, Mr & Mrs Blair, into their idyllic village home.[[72]](#cite_note-72) The film also stars [Mickey Sumner](/wiki/Mickey_Sumner "Mickey Sumner"), Ruben Crow, Regan Elizabeth Brown, Dave Mounfield and Aaron Davis.[[73]](#cite_note-73) Pearson received critical acclaim by critics for her performance as Mrs. Blair.[[74]](#cite_note-74) William of *All Horror* spoke most highly of Pearson's performance as Mrs. Blair, with him writing, "[Pearson] deserves some kind of award for her nearly wordless, but not silent performance. She lets the audience know with her eyes and grunts that this skin-suit is barely containing some other life-form that is dying to tear you apart, but cannot until she gets these irritating photographs from the couple."[[75]](#cite_note-75) Frank Scheck of *[The Hollywood Reporter](/wiki/The_Hollywood_Reporter "The Hollywood Reporter")* also spoke highly of such a thing, in which he stated: "[...] *Caught* delivers plenty of terrifying moments, thanks to the highly committed performances by the central quartet [...]".[[76]](#cite_note-76) Noel Murray of the *[Los Angeles Times](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times "Los Angeles Times")* noted praise of the performances, remarking that "*Caught* hits the usual beats, but with an unusually strong cast and original characters."[[77]](#cite_note-77) Jeannette Catsoulis praised the cast, in her review for *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")*, by stating "As played by [Barry] and [Pearson] [...], the interlopers are a weird, disgusting delight, easily dominating the movie and their ineffectual hosts."[[78]](#cite_note-78) Bobby LePire of *[Film Threat](/wiki/Film_Threat "Film Threat")* Bobby LePire wrote, "What cannot be overlooked is the animalistic qualities of Mrs. Blair.", in which he later concludes that "[...] the acting serves the unnerving atmosphere perfectly [...]".[[79]](#cite_note-79) [Brad Miska](/wiki/Brad_Miska "Brad Miska") of *[Bloody Disgusting](/wiki/Bloody_Disgusting "Bloody Disgusting")* acclaimed the performances of Barry and Pearson, with Miska stating, "There's plenty of tension, with the performances by [Barry] and [Pearson] carrying the entire film on their backs, not to mention an escalation of terror [...]".[[80]](#cite_note-80) Mike Sprague of *[JoBlo.com](/wiki/JoBlo.com "JoBlo.com")* praised Pearson's characterisation, in which he wrote, "[Pearson's] arc throughout the movie is my favorite thing about the piece, and I'm sure it will be yours as well."[[81]](#cite_note-81) *[Film at Lincoln Center](/wiki/Film_at_Lincoln_Center "Film at Lincoln Center")* described both Barry and Pearson's Mr and Mrs. Blair as being "played perfectly".[[82]](#cite_note-82)

Pearson's next appeared in Corrie Greenop's horror thriller *Dark Beacon* (2017).[[83]](#cite_note-83) She played Amy Wilcock, who loves the married Beth Gadbsy with a fierce and tragic passion.[[84]](#cite_note-84) The resulting performance was positively received; Sol Harris of *[Starburst](/wiki/Starburst_(magazine) "Starburst (magazine)")* concluded, "Pearson and Lynne Anne Rodgers do an admirable job working with the material that they’re given and attempting to carry the vast majority of the film on their own - not to mention child actor, Kendra Mei [...]".[[85]](#cite_note-85) Jon Dickinson of *[Scream](/wiki/Scream_(magazine) "Scream (magazine)")* wrote that, "In a post-*Skins* appearance, April Pearson is great in the lead role as Amy. She is the voice of reason when things start to get spooky."[[86]](#cite_note-86) April McIntyre of *HeyUGuys* praised Pearson by stating, "[Pearson] has emerged from a post-*Skins* hibernation with a strong and nuanced performance as Amy [...] Matched perfectly with Beth's spiralling psychosis, together they complement each other with an effective balance of wanting, sheer terror and anxiety."[[87]](#cite_note-87) For her work in *Dark Beacon*, Pearson received the award for Best Actress from American Horror Film Festival and the Upstate NY Horror Film Festival.[[88]](#cite_note-88)

The following year, it was announced that Pearson would portray Isla Crane in [Edgar Wallace](/wiki/Edgar_Wallace "Edgar Wallace")'s *The Case of the Frightened Lady*.[[89]](#cite_note-89)[[13]](#cite_note-Theatre_Royal_Editors-13) Response to Pearson's performance in *The Case of the Frightened Lady* was met with enthusiasm;[[90]](#cite_note-90) Vikki Stephenson of *Young Perspective* wrote, "[Pearson] proved adept as a terrified Isla (the secretary of the house) [...] These performances result in an ultimately enjoyable and engaging show."[[91]](#cite_note-91) Alison Brinkworth, writing for *Behind The Arras*, described her as adding "youthful vibrancy",[[92]](#cite_note-92) while Rebecca Lipkin described, in her review for *[The Arbuturian](/wiki/The_Arbuturian "The Arbuturian")*, Pearson as "charismatic".[[93]](#cite_note-93) Albeit praise for Pearson's performance, critics highlighted the underwritten characterisation of Pearson's Isla Crane.[[94]](#cite_note-94)[[95]](#cite_note-95)

She reunited with *Skins* co-creator Bryan Elsley for the third time, in which she briefly appeared as Polly in *[Kiss Me First](/wiki/Kiss_Me_First_(TV_series) "Kiss Me First (TV series)")*.[[96]](#cite_note-96) Pearson also starred as Lily in Patterson's critically acclaimed [comedy-drama](/wiki/Comedy-drama "Comedy-drama") *Tucked*  (2018), the estranged daughter of [Derren Nesbitt](/wiki/Derren_Nesbitt "Derren Nesbitt")'s Jackie.[[97]](#cite_note-97)[[98]](#cite_note-98)[[99]](#cite_note-99) In 2018, Pearson co-wrote and starred in Patterson's [independent](/wiki/Independent_film "Independent film") [comedy-drama](/wiki/Comedy-drama "Comedy-drama") film *Tracked*, in which a young couple try to save their failing relationship by interrailing around Europe together.[[100]](#cite_note-100)[[101]](#cite_note-101)[[102]](#cite_note-102) The film premiered at the [Raindance Film Festival](/wiki/Raindance_Film_Festival "Raindance Film Festival"),[[103]](#cite_note-103) wherein Pearson and co-star Chris Willoughby's performances were praised, as well as Edouard Fousset's cinematography.[[104]](#cite_note-104)[[105]](#cite_note-105) In 2020, she also appeared as Katy in [supernatural](/wiki/Supernatural_fiction "Supernatural fiction") [rom-com](/wiki/Romantic_comedy "Romantic comedy") short film *One Year Later*, with whom she co-starred alongside starring [Simon Weir](/wiki/Simon_Weir "Simon Weir"), Mark Wood, and Rhys-Teare Williams.[[106]](#cite_note-106) She was also amongst the first to sign up for [George Webster](/wiki/George_Webster_(actor) "George Webster (actor)")'s British [science fiction comedy](/wiki/Science_fiction_comedy "Science fiction comedy") *Star Dogs*, wherein she played Cass Rio.[[107]](#cite_note-107) Pearson also appeared as Abi in [Matthew Leutwyler](/wiki/Matthew_Leutwyler "Matthew Leutwyler") and Anton Laines' *Disconnected*, which was a weekly, experimental drama series shot by the actors and produced remotely, in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), [India](/wiki/India "India"), [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), and [Rwanda](/wiki/Rwanda "Rwanda"), while under countrywide quarantine orders due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").[[108]](#cite_note-108)[[109]](#cite_note-109)[[110]](#cite_note-110) In March 2021, she appeared in an episode of the BBC soap opera *[Doctors](/wiki/Doctors_(2000_TV_series) "Doctors (2000 TV series)")* as [Chloe Shapley](/wiki/List_of_Doctors_characters_(2021)#Chloe_Shapley "List of Doctors characters (2021)").[[111]](#cite_note-111)

Pearson stars as Eva in Adam Oldroyd's [black comedy](/wiki/Black_comedy "Black comedy") directorial debut *Sideshow*, alongside [Anthony Stewart Head](/wiki/Anthony_Stewart_Head "Anthony Stewart Head"), [Les Dennis](/wiki/Les_Dennis "Les Dennis"), and [Nathan Clarke](/wiki/Nathan_Clarke_(actor) "Nathan Clarke (actor)").[[112]](#cite_note-112) Additionally, she reunited with director Jamie Patterson in the [romantic](/wiki/Romance_film "Romance film") [crime film](/wiki/Crime_film "Crime film") *God's Petting You*,[[113]](#cite_note-113) and in the [psychological thriller](/wiki/Psychological_thriller "Psychological thriller") *The Kindred*.[[114]](#cite_note-Kindred-114)[[115]](#cite_note-115)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=April_Pearson&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Pearson was involved in [Channel 4](/wiki/Channel_4 "Channel 4")'s "Lost for Words" campaign, a season of campaigning programmes to get children reading, in which she reads aloud from *Kasper in the Glitter* by [Philip Ridley](/wiki/Philip_Ridley "Philip Ridley").[[116]](#cite_note-116)[[117]](#cite_note-117) She's an admirer of [Spanish cinema](/wiki/Cinema_of_Spain "Cinema of Spain") and the works of [Pedro Almodóvar](/wiki/Pedro_Almod%C3%B3var "Pedro Almodóvar"),[[118]](#cite_note-Almodóvar-118) while speaking highly of [Malorie Blackman](/wiki/Malorie_Blackman "Malorie Blackman")'s [*Noughts and Crosses* series](/wiki/Noughts_%26_Crosses_series "Noughts & Crosses series").[[118]](#cite_note-Almodóvar-118) She's based in [Brighton](/wiki/Brighton "Brighton").[[119]](#cite_note-Brighton-119) In 2014, Pearson founded the Laboratory Theatre Company with film director Jamie Patterson.[[120]](#cite_note-120) The Laboratory Theatre is a production company dedicated to blending theatre and film.[[119]](#cite_note-Brighton-119) Pearson began a relationship with Patterson, after they met on the set of the *Home for Christmas* (2014).[[121]](#cite_note-121) Pearson and Patterson were married in 2017.[[122]](#cite_note-122) Since 2020, Pearson has hosted a casual celebrity interview-based podcast called *Are you Michelle from Skins?*;[[123]](#cite_note-123) the first episode being recorded on 6 July and broadcast on [Instagram](/wiki/Instagram "Instagram").[[124]](#cite_note-124) She listed her idols as including [Bernadette Peters](/wiki/Bernadette_Peters "Bernadette Peters"), [Nicole Kidman](/wiki/Nicole_Kidman "Nicole Kidman"), [Julie Walters](/wiki/Julie_Walters "Julie Walters"), and [Judi Dench](/wiki/Judi_Dench "Judi Dench").[[27]](#cite_note-Tormented-27)[[125]](#cite_note-125) On 8 December 2021, Pearson announced she was pregnant with her first child.[[126]](#cite_note-126)

She resides in [Brighton](/wiki/Brighton "Brighton").

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=April_Pearson&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-Party_131-0)** The *Skins* "mini-sode" was broadcast after the conclusion of the first series, bridging the gap between the first and second series. It was directed by [Owen Harris](/wiki/Owen_Harris_(director) "Owen Harris (director)"), with the short featuring the characters organising a party, with live music from *[Foals](/wiki/Foals_(band) "Foals (band)")*.[[127]](#cite_note-127)[[128]](#cite_note-Myspace-128) Filming took part in a cavernous old church in [Bristol](/wiki/Bristol "Bristol") and was broadcast on [Myspace](/wiki/Myspace "Myspace").[[129]](#cite_note-129)[[130]](#cite_note-130)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-Unseen_135-0)** The *Skins* series was a variety of short online specials that ran in conjunction with the main series, featuring new young writers and directors.[[131]](#cite_note-132) Pearson first appeared in *I Mostly Do* on 26 January 2007, which was directed by [Nicholas Hoult](/wiki/Nicholas_Hoult "Nicholas Hoult"), and subsequently at least four episodes afterwards, including: "A Friend In Need", "Careers Advice II", "The Cat & The Duck", and "Michelle's Looking for a Date".[[132]](#cite_note-133)[[133]](#cite_note-134)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-Lost_138-0)** The *Skins:The Lost Weeks* were broadcast after the conclusion of the first series, bridging the gap between the first and second series.[[134]](#cite_note-136)[[135]](#cite_note-137)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-Three_141-0)** After its various runs at [Brighton Fringe](/wiki/Brighton_Fringe "Brighton Fringe") and the [Edinburgh Festival Fringe](/wiki/Edinburgh_Festival_Fringe "Edinburgh Festival Fringe") in 2015, *Threesome* was transferred to [Union Theatre, London](/wiki/Union_Theatre,_London "Union Theatre, London") from 3 May, to 14 May 2016,[[136]](#cite_note-139) and later returned to the Union Theatre from 14 March, to 25 March 2017.[[137]](#cite_note-140)
5. **[^](#cite_ref-Frightened_145-0)** After opening at the [Theatre Royal, Windsor](/wiki/Theatre_Royal,_Windsor "Theatre Royal, Windsor") on 10 January 2018,[[138]](#cite_note-142) *The Case of the Frightened Lady* was followed by a nationwide tour.[[139]](#cite_note-143)[[140]](#cite_note-144)
